data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was triggered by a pandemic of a highly virulent, zoonotic disease that spread along the very trade and diplomatic networks that defined the interconnected palace system, selectively collapsing states with high elite density.", "mechanism": "The intensive palace complexes, with their dense administrative staffs, storage facilities (attracting rodents), and constant diplomatic gift-exchange, created an ideal epidemiological environment. A pathogen (e.g., a hemorrhagic fever) introduced via trade from a new ecological zone swept through the Eastern Mediterranean. It killed a critical mass of the literate administrative class, rendering the complex redistributive economy non-functional. The collapse was simultaneous because the disease transmission followed the speed of ship travel and diplomatic contact.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Paleopathological analysis of skeletal remains from destruction layers shows no evidence of a novel pathogen or mass mortality event distinct from endemic disease.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Contemporary texts from multiple sites describe a prolonged famine and enemy invasion, with no mention of a plague affecting the ruling class.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Egypt survived because its more dispersed rural population along the Nile and its less concentrated administrative structure (with provincial nomarchs) provided a buffer against elite decapitation. The pandemic hypothesis explains the eerie silence in the archaeological record—people didn't rebuild because the institutional knowledge died with the scribes."}
